segments.s319-g49: 'پس آنگه' is a poetic duplication meaning 'then' (temporal sequencing). 'نشست' is the simple past, 3rd person singular of نشستن, 'he sat.' The phrase 'از بر تخت عاج' uses the classical compound preposition 'از بر' (here functioning as a locative/locutional marker indicating position on/at) followed by the noun phrase 'تخت عاج' = 'throne (تخت) of ivory (عاج)'. Word order is verb then locative phrase.
segments.s319-g50: 'به سر' is a locative prepositional phrase meaning 'onto the head.' 'برنهاد' is a classical/poetic perfective past of نهادن with the prefix بر- (برنهاد = 'placed upon/laid on'), 3rd person singular. 'آن' is a demonstrative ('that'/'the') pointing to the following noun phrase. 'دل‌افروز تاج' is a compound adjective + noun ('heart-delighting crown'); note Persian permits the adjective to follow or precede depending on style—here it forms a unit describing the crown.
segments.s319-g51: The subject 'سپاه' ('host, army') is followed by the compound predicate 'انجمن شد' literally 'became an assembly' (شد is past tense 'became'), so the phrase means 'the host gathered.' 'به درگاه شاه' is a locative phrase ('at/to the king's court/presence'); درگاه here denotes the royal court.
segments.s319-g52: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing who formed the assembly. 'ردان' is an older/poetic noun placed in apposition to the following nouns; it functions as part of the subject group. 'و' = 'and' links the items. 'بزرگان' is the plural 'great ones' (nobles). 'زرین کلاه' is a modifier-noun sequence meaning 'golden-capped' (زرین = golden; کلاه = cap/hat). The phrase as a whole describes the assembled nobles wearing golden caps.
segments.s319-g53: 'به شاهی' uses the preposition به plus the abstract noun شاهی ('kingship') to mark the object/goal of the acclamation — literally 'to (his) kingship.' 'بر او آفرین خواندند' is a fixed expression: 'آفرین خواندن' = 'to utter acclamations' (خواندند is past, 3rd person plural). 'بر او' ('upon him') indicates the recipient of the acclamation.
segments.s319-g54: 'بر آن تاج' = 'upon that crown' (بر + demonstrative + noun). The clause continues with 'بر گوهر افشاندند' where 'افشاندند' is past, 3rd person plural of افشانیدن 'to scatter/sprinkle'; here it means they sprinkled/strewed gems ('گوهر') upon the crown. The doubled use of بر before گوهر is a poetic ordering marking the target ('upon it') and the object scattered.
segments.s319-g55: 'یکی' is the indefinite article-like word 'a/certain' introducing the event. 'سور' = 'feast/banquet.' The adjective 'بد' follows and qualifies the feast in an older/poetic usage (here intensifying or characterizing the feast). 'در جهان' = 'in the world' and the idiom 'سر به سر' means 'throughout' or 'all over' (reduplication 'head to head' used adverbially). Together the phrase describes a feast celebrated throughout the world.
segments.s319-g56: 'چو' here is the subordinating conjunction 'when.' The clause 'بر تخت بنشست' is past tense ('he sat upon the throne') with the prefix ب- on نشست indicating perfective past 'sat down.' 'پیروزگر' follows as a predicate/epithet meaning 'the victorious one' (here a nominative label for the person who sat). The whole subordinate clause sets the temporal condition: 'when the victorious sat on the throne.'
segments.s319-g25: همه is a quantifier placed before the noun (’all’); جامه‌هاشان is the plural noun جامه 'garment' with the possessive suffix -شان 'their'. The adjectives کبود و سیاه follow the noun, a common Persian order (noun + modifiers), describing the garments' colors.
segments.s319-g26: دو هفته is an adverbial duration ('two weeks') that precedes the verb. ببودند is an older/literary past plural form meaning 'they were' or 'they remained' (ب‑ prefix appears in classical Persian). با سوگ شاه ('with the mourning of the king') gives the reason/circumstance: 'in mourning for the king.'
segments.s319-g27: ز بهر is a fixed classical-prepositional sequence meaning 'for the sake of' or 'because of'; ستودانش here functions as 'his praise' (the noun/masdar sense) with the enclitic -ش 'his'. کاخی بلند is a noun phrase 'a lofty palace' (کاخ + the adjectival/qualifying بلند after the noun).
segments.s319-g28: بکردند is the past plural verb 'they made/did/erected' (classical past). بالای او is a locative phrase 'on top of it/him' (بالای + pronoun او). ده کمند is a quantified noun phrase 'ten کمند' serving as the direct object/thing they set up; word order places verb before the locative and object in this poetic sentence.
segments.s319-g29: ببردند is a past plural verb 'they carried' (classical form). پس is an adverb meaning 'then/afterwards' and precedes the subject; نامداران شاه 'the king's famed/renowned men' is the subject phrase (نامداران with the genitive/possessive relation to شاه).
segments.s319-g30: This is a coordinated noun phrase naming the goods taken: دبیقی and دیبای رومی سیاه. دبیقی here names a fine fabric (sense: fine fabric), دیبای is brocade/silk, رومی qualifies it as 'Roman/Byzantine' and سیاه is the color 'black'—adjectives follow the nouns as modifiers.
segments.s319-g31: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon it/him' (بر + pronoun او). The list تافته عود و کافور و مشک are material/noun items placed on/over that receptacle: تافته (a woven/taffeta cloth), عود (agarwood/oud), کافور (camphor) and مشک (musk). Coordination with و connects the aromatic materials.
segments.s319-g32: تنش را is the definite direct object 'his body' (تن + possessive -ش plus object marker را). بدو is a dative/clitic pronoun 'to it' (referring back to the receptacle mentioned), در is the preposition 'in', and بکردند is the past plural verb 'they put/did'. خشک is a resultative adjective 'dry' describing the result (they placed his body in it and made it dry—i.e., embalmed/treated). The elements combine as object + dative + locative + verb + result.
segments.s319-g17: Subject–predicate order: همان سرو (that same cypress) is the subject, یازنده شد is a past copular construction where شد functions as 'became' and یازنده is a predicative noun/epithet (poetic/archaic). The simile introduced by چون + noun (چون کمان) follows the verb and modifies the whole predicate: 'became like a bow.'
segments.s319-g18: ندارم is first‑person present negative ('I do not have'). The phrase گران‌گر here functions as a substantive/adjective ('an exalted/honouring one' — a poetic epithet) and سرآید زمان is a temporal clause ('when the time comes') placed after it; the whole clause means 'I have no exalted one when the time arrives.'
segments.s319-g19: بسی is an adverbial quantifier ('much/very many') used here with the negative verb برنیامد to express scarcity or failure to appear — literally 'much did not appear' → 'few came forward.' برنیامد is past negative/negative perfective meaning 'did not appear/succeed,' and بر این روزگار places this action 'in/against these times.'
segments.s319-g20: کزو = 'from/of whom' links back to those who did (or did not) appear; ماند is simple past 'remained' (resultative); نام here means 'name' in the sense of reputation/fame, and از جهان یادگار is a prepositional phrase 'as a memorial to the world' — together: 'from whom a name remained as the world's memorial.'
segments.s319-g21: Title + name (جهاندار کیخسرو) is the subject; آمد is simple past 'came'; به گاه ('to/at the time') is an adverbial expression meaning 'at the appointed time/when due.' The order is canonical subject–verb–time adjunct.
segments.s319-g22: نشست is past 'sat' or 'took his seat.' The phrase از بر زیرگه is a locative phrase: از بر + زیرگه (a low place/low platform) here describes where he sat (on the low bench/dais). با سپاه ('with the army') is an accompaniment phrase closing the clause. Word order places the verb before its locative and accompaniment complements, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s319-g23: از ایرانیان is a partitive/genitive phrase 'of the Iranians.' هرکه is an indefinite relative 'whoever.' بد here modifies نامجوی (a compound noun meaning 'name‑seeker' = someone seeking fame), so بد نامجوی describes 'a bad/ill‑reputed fame‑seeker.' The sequence describes which subset of Iranians is meant.
segments.s319-g24: پیاده is adverbial here 'on foot' (or adjectival 'foot‑borne'); برفتند is past plural 'they went/they departed.' The predicates بی‌رنگ و بوی are postpositive descriptive phrases 'without color and scent' (idiomatically 'pale and scentless' / lifeless), describing their condition as they left.
segments.s319-g33: نهادند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb 'they placed/put'. زیر اندرش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'beneath him' where زیر = 'under' and اندر + ش (اندرش) yields 'in/under + his' (archaic enclitic pronominal). تخت عاج is a noun phrase 'throne of ivory' with the material/quality noun عاج following تخت.
segments.s319-g34: به سر بر is a locative framing 'on/onto the head' (به + سر, then بر reinforcing 'on'). ز introduces the material 'of/from' (poetic ز), so ز کافور ... مشک qualifies تاج as made of/with camphor and musk; وز here functions as a poetic conjunction 'and (from)' joining the two materials. تاج is the head noun 'crown'.
segments.s319-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برگشت is a past verb 'returned' and appears before the name (inverted order typical of epic style). کیخسرو is the subject (proper name 'Kay Khosrow'). از پیش تخت is a prepositional phrase 'from before the throne' with پیش = 'before/in the presence of' and از = 'from'.
segments.s319-g36: در here is 'door/gate' (not the preposition) and خوابگه is 'sleeping-place/chamber', followed by the object marker را. ببستند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they closed/shut' and سخت functions adverbially 'firmly / tightly'. The sequence 'در خوابگه را ببستند سخت' => 'they shut the door of the sleeping-chamber tightly.'
segments.s319-g37: کسی نیز ... را ندید is negative in sense: 'no one else saw ...'. کاووس کی is the name with the title element کی ('Kai') following the personal name — together 'Kai Kavus' — and را is the definite object marker attached to the name. ندید is the past 'saw' (here negated by context: 'no one saw').
segments.s319-g38: ز introduces causes or sources 'from/because of' (poetic), so ز کین و ز آوردگاه reads 'from hatred and from the battlefield'. آرمید is the past verb 'he rested/ceased' referring back to the named king. The sentence links the two clauses: no one saw him; he rested, worn by hatred and war.
segments.s319-g39: چنینست is a copular/existential formula 'thus it is / such is'. رسم is the noun 'custom' or 'rule'. سرای سپنج is a genitive phrase 'the house/abode (سرای) of سپنج'; here سرای functions as a dependent noun ('house/household/shop') modifying رسم. (Note: سپنج is literal 'sponge' but may be a conventional name/epithet in context.)
segments.s319-g40: نمانی is from ماندن 'to remain' with a negative/imperative coloring here 'do not remain' or 'do not stay' (classical subjunctive/imperative form). در او = 'in it' (در = in/within; او = it/he, here referring back to the house/custom). جاودانه = 'forever/eternal' qualifying the duration. مرنج is an independent negative imperative 'do not be grieved / do not take offense' directed at the addressee.
segments.s319-g41: The clause begins with the negative particle نه (forming the first half of a neither–nor pair). دانا is a noun used generically ('the wise one' or 'wise people'). گذر یابد is an archaic/poetic word order: گذر (passage/escape) + یابد (present/subjunctive form of یافتن used here as 'attain/escape'), so literally 'the wise attain passage' — with نه negating it: 'the wise do not escape.' از introduces the source ('from/out of'), چنگ مرگ ('the claw/grip of death') is a possessive/compound image; note the verb is present-subjunctive/optative in form (یابد) rather than a simple indicative.
segments.s319-g42: The second negative clause repeats نه and omits the verb (elliptical: it inherits the sense of 'do not escape' from the previous clause). جنگ‌آوران is the plural subject 'warriors.' زیر is the preposition 'under/beneath' and places the following nouns in a locative/covering sense; خفتان here refers to the sleeping/dead (a poetic plural/nominal form of 'to sleep' → 'those who sleep' / sleep-shroud), و links to ترگ 'rags' (burial shrouds or rags). The clause therefore pictures warriors lying beneath shrouds and rags (i.e., subject to death).
segments.s319-g43: اگر ... باشی is a conditional clause: اگر introduces 'if', شاه is the predicate noun 'king' and باشی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of بودن ('to be') used here as 'are.' وگر is a coordinating conditional 'or/but if' introducing an alternate identity; زردهشت is the proper name Zardešt (Zoroaster) used as the second alternative. The structure means 'if you are king, or if you are Zoroaster.'
segments.s319-g44: نهالی (a young plant/sapling) is used metaphorically for a fragile human life. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' indicating origin (َنهالی ز خاکست = 'a sapling from ash'). خاکست means 'ashes.' و links the next phrase; بالین here means 'pillow' (literal or metaphorical bedside). The second ز again marks material/origin: بالین ز خشت = 'a pillow of brick' (i.e., humble, hard resting-place). The copula 'to be' is omitted — the noun phrases describe your condition.
segments.s319-g45: چنان دان is an imperative: 'know/consider thus.' که introduces a content clause ('that'). گیتی is the grammatical subject 'the world,' ترا is the accusative/oblique 2nd-person pronoun 'to you' (object/beneficiary), and دشمنست is the predicate 'is an enemy' (دشمن + -ست copula). Word order is verbal/poetic: consider that the world stands as an enemy to you.
segments.s319-g46: This is a short equative/metaphorical construction where predicates follow the nouns: زمین بستر = 'the earth (is) bed' and گور پیراهنست = 'the grave (is) shirt.' The copula is expressed as -ست on پیراهنst in the second half; the first half omits an explicit copula (typical in Persian verbless equatives). The line gives two metaphors: earth as bed, grave as clothing.
segments.s319-g47: چهل روز is a duration phrase 'forty days' placed before the verb for emphasis. سوگ نیا here is a compound meaning 'mourning (for the ancestors / ancestral mourning)' or more generally 'a formal mourning rite'; داشت is the past-tense verb 'had/kept,' and شاه is the subject 'the king.' The word order (duration + object + verb + subject) is poetic/inverted but common in classical Persian.
segments.s319-g48: ز شادی شده دور is a participial/adjectival phrase: ز (from) شادی (joy) + شده (become/done) + دور (distant/removed) → 'he became distant from joy' or 'he withdrew from joy.' وز here is a fused form representing و + ز ('and from'), introducing the next prepositional phrase; تاج و گاه = 'the crown and the court/time/place' (i.e. crown and royal seat). Note that وز in the manuscript is a contraction (and+from) rather than the separate lexical senses listed, so its sense in the line is 'and from.'
segments.s319-g9: ز تو خواستم = a light prepositional phrase + past verb: ز (from/of) + تو (you) + خواستم (1st person past of خواستن) = 'I asked of you' or 'I requested you'. تا introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that') and governs a subjunctive clause. یکی کینه‌ور = 'a certain / someone' (یکی) followed by the adjective کینه‌ور (vindictive), functioning as the indefinite person to be sent/used.
segments.s319-g10: به کین سیاوش ببندد کمر is the purposive clause governed by تا. به + کین = 'for/with (a) hatred' (purpose/means). سیاوش is the target of that hatred. ببندد is 3rd person singular present/subjunctive (the form used after تا for an optative/purposive sense) 'he may gird / he should gird'. کمر ('waist') is the object of بندیدن in the idiom کمر بستن = 'to gird one's loins / prepare oneself'; the slightly inverted order (verb before کمر) is poetic but the sense is the usual idiom.
segments.s319-g11: نبیره بدیدم is the main clause: نبیره (scion/heir) is the direct object and بدیدم is 1st person past. In older/fixed uses بدیدم can mean 'I gave/appointed' or literally 'I saw'; here it introduces the person appointed/recognized. جهانبین is a postpositive adjective 'world-wise / worldly-seeing' modifying نبیره, and خویش is a possessive/reflexive element ('his/its own' or in context 'my/one's own') attached to خوی (disposition); together they mark that the scion is 'of his/her own disposition' or 'my own'.
segments.s319-g12: به introduces the measure/quality ('in/by/with'). The sequence فرهنگ و تدبیر و آیین is a coordinated list of qualities (culture, counsel/prudence, custom/practice); خویش (here written as خوی + ش) is the possessive/reflexive attached to the final noun, binding the whole list to the same possessor ('of his/own'). The structure therefore describes the scion as sharing those qualities.
segments.s319-g13: جهانجوی is the subject ('world-seeker' / ambitious person). با introduces an instrumental/attendant list ('with'); فر, برز, خرد are three coordinated nouns giving the means/qualities (فر = royal glory, برز = exaltation/highness, خرد = wisdom). The noun list after با is a typical Persian coordintion linked by و.
segments.s319-g14: ز شاهان پیشینگان is the comparative/ablative phrase introduced by ز ('from/out of' → 'beyond / than the kings of earlier times'); شاهان پیشینگان = 'the kings of the former age' (شاهان 'kings' modified by پیشینگان 'those who came before'). بگذرد is 3rd person singular present/subjunctive used in narrative to mean 'passes/exceeds' (= 'will surpass').
segments.s319-g15: چو = 'when' (temporal clause). سالم here is the proper name Salm (classical spelling سالم). سه پنجاه is the archaic numeral order meaning 'three and fifty' = 53 (unit before tens). بر سر گذشت is an idiom 'passed over his head' meaning the years passed him / he reached that age; گذشت is past tense 'passed'.
segments.s319-g16: سر موی is a genitive phrase 'the hair of the head' (سر = head, موی = hair). مشکین is an adjective (poetic) applied to موی — here best read as 'musk‑scented / perfumed' or possibly 'dark' depending on context; چو = 'like/as' introduces the simile, کافور = camphor, and گشت (past of گشتن) = 'became' (verb placed at the end in normal Persian order).
segments.s319-g1: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). با ایمنی گشت is an adverbial + past verb: با (with) + ایمنی (safety) + گشت (became) = 'became safe/secure'. Classical word order places the verb (گشت) before or adjacent to the subject; کاووس is the subject 'Kavus'. جفت follows to indicate 'the pair / the two of them' (acting together) and will be the logical subject for the following verb.
segments.s319-g2: همه راز دل = a noun phrase 'all the secrets of the heart' (همه 'all' + راز 'secret' governed by دل 'heart'). پیش یزدان = prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before God' (پیش 'before/in the presence of' + یزدان 'God'). بگفت is a literary past-tense form 'said/told' (3rd person); in classical usage this simple past can cover 'he/they told'.
segments.s319-g3: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین 'thus/so' modifying گفت). کای is a vocative 'O' (archaic/classical); برتر از روزگار is a comparative phrase 'higher/superior than fate/time' (برتر 'superior' + از روزگار 'than fate/time'). The clause addresses the addressee with a lofty epithet.
segments.s319-g4: تو باشی is an optative/wish construction where باشی (present-subjunctive form) expresses 'may you be / you should be'. به هر نیکی is a prepositional phrase 'in/to every goodness' (به + هر نیکی). آموزگار is the predicative noun 'teacher / guide', so the whole gives a benediction: 'may you be a teacher of every virtue.'
segments.s319-g5: ز تو یافتم = 'From you I obtained' (ز 'from' in poetic register + تو 'you' + یافتم 'I obtained/received' — past tense). فر و اورنگ و بخت lists three related possessions: فر (royal splendour/glory), اورنگ (worldly rank/throne/glory), and بخت (fortune/fate). The coordinating و links the nouns as items received from the addressee.
segments.s319-g6: بزرگی و دیهیم و هم تاج و تخت continues the list of honors: بزرگی 'greatness'. دیهیم is an archaic/rare word here inserted among honorific terms (its precise classical sense is uncertain in modern vernacular). هم 'also/and' links to تاج و تخت 'crown and throne' as further acquisitions. The sequence is a pile-up of favors/possessions obtained.
segments.s319-g7: تو کردی کسی را = 'you made someone' (تو 'you' + کردی 'you did/made' + کسی 'someone' followed by the object marker را). چو من = 'like me' (چو 'as/like' + من 'me') modifies کسی, and بهرمند is the resulting predicate 'benefited/enjoying' — together: 'you made someone like me a beneficiary.'
segments.s319-g8: ز گنج و ز تخت و ز نام بلند = three repeating prepositional phrases introduced by the poetic ز 'from/of', each naming what the person enjoys: گنج (treasure), تخت (throne), and نام بلند ('high/lofty name' = exalted reputation). The repeated ز gives a liturgical, emphatic listing of benefits.

sentences.s319-s25: Then he sat upon the ivory throne; upon his head he set that heart-delighting crown.
sentences.s319-s26: The host gathered at the king's court — courtiers and the great men in golden caps.
sentences.s319-s27: They cried 'Hail to his kingship!' and strewed jewels upon that crown.
sentences.s319-s28: A great feast was held throughout the world when the victorious one sat upon the throne.
sentences.s319-s13: All their garments were dark blue and black; they remained in mourning for the king for two weeks.
sentences.s319-s14: For the sake of praising him they raised a lofty palace; atop it they made ten kemands.
sentences.s319-s15: Then the king's renowned men carried off Dabiqi and Roman black brocade.
sentences.s319-s16: Upon it they laid taffeta, agarwood, camphor and musk; they put his body into it and made it dry.
sentences.s319-s9: That same cypress became like a bow; I have no exalted one when the time comes.
sentences.s319-s10: Very few in these times came forward; from them a name remained as a memorial in the world.
sentences.s319-s11: The sovereign Kay Khosrow came at the appointed hour and sat upon the low dais with his army.
sentences.s319-s12: Of the Iranians, whoever was a bad fame‑seeker departed on foot, pale and without scent.
sentences.s319-s17: They set an ivory throne beneath him and placed on his head a crown of camphor and musk.
sentences.s319-s18: When Kay Khosrow returned from before the throne, they firmly shut the door of the sleeping-chamber.
sentences.s319-s19: No one else saw Kai Kavus; weary of hate and the battlefield, he rested.
sentences.s319-s20: Such is the custom of the house of song; do not remain in it forever — be not sorrowful.
sentences.s319-s21: Neither the wise escape the grasp of death, nor do the warriors avoid ending up under shrouds and rags.
sentences.s319-s22: If you are a king or even Zoroaster, you are a sapling sprung from ashes and your pillow is of brick.
sentences.s319-s23: Know this: the world is hostile to you; the earth is a bed and the grave a shirt.
sentences.s319-s24: The king observed forty days of mourning; he withdrew from joy, and from the crown and the court.
sentences.s319-s5: I asked you to have a certain vindictive man gird his loins in hatred (vengeance) against Siavash.
sentences.s319-s6: I set (or saw) a scion — a world-wise one — as my own, in my culture, counsel, and custom.
sentences.s319-s7: The world-seeker, with glory, exaltation, and wisdom, will surpass the kings of former times.
sentences.s319-s8: When Salm had passed fifty-three years, the hair on his head became musk‑scented like camphor.
sentences.s319-s1: When Kavus became safe, the two of them told all the secrets of their hearts before God.
sentences.s319-s2: Thus He said: 'O you who are above fate, may you be a teacher in every goodness.'
sentences.s319-s3: From you I have received glory and worldly rank and fortune, greatness and dihim, and likewise crown and throne.
sentences.s319-s4: You made someone like me a beneficiary of treasure, of throne, and of high renown.
